description: This figure presents a multi-panel line graph showing the effect of aluminum content (3–10 wt.%) on the mechanical properties of Fe-Mn-Al-C austenitic steels containing 0.85% C (dashed curves) and 0.95% C (solid curves), thermally processed by water quenching from 1150°C followed by aging at 550°C for 16 h. The top panel plots mechanical strength: solid and open triangles represent ultimate tensile strength (UTS) and 0.2% yield strength (0.2% YS), respectively. Both UTS and YS remain relatively flat below approximately 7% Al but rise sharply at higher Al contents, with the 0.95% C alloy consistently outperforming the 0.85% C alloy. The middle panel displays ductility, where solid circles denote elongation (δ) and open circles denote reduction of area (ψ); both metrics stay fairly stable across most of the Al range before declining slightly above approximately 8% Al. The bottom panel shows U-notch impact toughness (K_CU) with solid and open diamonds corresponding to 0.85% C and 0.95% C, respectively; toughness peaks near 6–7% Al (approximately 180 J/cm² for 0.85% C and 160 J/cm² for 0.95% C) and then decreases at higher Al contents. The figure supports the conclusion that considerable age hardening occurs when Al exceeds approximately 7%, attributed to homogeneous κ-phase precipitation, whereas lower Al contents lead to grain-boundary carbide networks detrimental to toughness, thereby defining a compositional window for balanced structural performance.

This optical micrograph displays the microstructure of an Fe-Mn-Al-C austenitic steel containing 4.6 wt.% Al after aging at 600°C for 16 h, with a scale bar of 20 μm. The image reveals polygonal austenite grains bounded by a prominent continuous or semi-continuous dark phase network along the grain boundaries, accompanied by finer intragranular particles and occasional plate-like precipitates within the matrix. The figure caption states this is κ-phase precipitation, whereas the accompanying paper text reports that for low aluminum content the carbide phase precipitates during aging as a network along grain boundaries (Fig. 2), which is highly detrimental to impact toughness. This micrograph thus provides direct metallographic evidence of the embrittling grain-boundary precipitation regime that must be excluded when establishing composition limits for structural steels. It supports the central scientific conclusion that aluminum contents below approximately 6.2 wt.% promote unacceptable grain-boundary carbide networks upon aging, in contrast to the homogeneous precipitation field sought at higher aluminum concentrations.

Figure 3 comprises two optical micrographs contrasting δ-ferrite morphology in Fe-Mn-Al-C austenitic steel containing 10.5 wt.% Al. Subfigure (a) shows the as-cast condition, where δ-ferrite appears as a dark, interconnected network distributed within the lighter austenite matrix. Subfigure (b) shows the microstructure after deformation, revealing polygonal austenite grains with visible grain boundaries and annealing twins; the δ-ferrite phase has been fragmented into discontinuous dark segments primarily located along grain boundaries, no longer forming the continuous as-cast network. Both micrographs include a 30 μm scale bar. The caption states that δ-ferrite appears when aluminum content exceeds 10 wt.%, and the accompanying text reports that this phase decreases technological plasticity and enhances mechanical anisotropy. Visually, the comparison demonstrates that deformation processing fragments the brittle as-cast ferrite network; the text further reports that hot working at 1150–1170°C can dissolve δ-ferrite for Al contents up to approximately 12%. Together, these observations support the paper's conclusion that composition and processing must be controlled to suppress δ-ferrite in high-Al austenitic steels for structural applications.

Figure 4 presents two optical micrographs comparing the quenched microstructure of granulated and pressed Fe-Mn-Al-C austenitic steel after quenching from 1100°C, with aluminum contents of (a) 9.8% and (b) 14%. Panel (a) displays a relatively homogeneous austenitic grain structure lacking visible δ-ferrite, while panel (b) clearly exhibits δ-ferrite precipitates (labeled δ and indicated by an arrow) located predominantly at grain boundaries within the austenite matrix. Both images include a 30 μm scale bar. The comparison illustrates that raising the aluminum content from 9.8% to 14% stabilizes the ferritic phase; the caption and associated text report that δ-ferrite may reach approximately 10 vol.% at 14% Al even in rapidly solidified material. This microstructural evidence supports the paper’s core conclusion that aluminum must be strictly limited (to about 9–9.5% for industrial ingots) to prevent embrittling δ-ferrite formation and ensure acceptable ultimate tensile strength and impact toughness for structural applications.

Figure 5 shows the dependence of saturation magnetization 4πJ_s (in Gauss) on carbon content (0.8–1.06 wt%) for Fe-Mn-Al-C austenitic steels with fixed 9% Al and 30% Mn, aged at temperatures between 350°C and 550°C for 16 h (dashed curves) and 100 h (solid curves). The plot comprises ten curves labeled by aging temperature and duration. For 100 h aging, 4πJ_s increases markedly with carbon content and saturates above approximately 0.92–0.96 wt% C, reaching plateau values near 2200–2400 G, with the 400°C/100h, 450°C/100h and 500°C/100h conditions displaying the highest magnetization. For 16 h aging, magnetization rises with both carbon content and aging temperature, remaining near zero at 350°C but reaching approximately 1600–1800 G at 550°C. At 550°C, the 16 h and 100 h curves nearly converge, indicating that kappa-phase precipitation kinetics are accelerated at this temperature. The caption and citation context indicate that carbon, as the most diffusion-mobile element, is the leading component at the onset of aging, and the measured saturation magnetization provides qualitative information on the intensity of kappa-phase precipitation during austenite aging.

This figure presents a three-panel line graph showing the effect of carbon content (0.3–1.1 wt.%) on the mechanical properties of Fe-Mn-Al-C austenitic steel with fixed 9% Al and 30% Mn. The top panel plots ultimate tensile strength (UTS, solid triangles) and 0.2% offset yield strength (0.2% YS, open triangles) in MPa; the middle panel shows ductility via reduction of area (ψ, open circles) and elongation (δ, solid circles) in percent; the bottom panel gives U-notch impact toughness (K_CU, open diamonds) in J/cm². Solid curves denote the aged condition (quenched from 1050°C then aged at 550°C for 16 h), while dashed curves represent the as-quenched state. All strength metrics increase monotonically with carbon, with aged samples showing higher values. Ductility and impact toughness both peak near 0.7–0.8 wt.% C (about 50% ductility and 120 J/cm² K_CU) before declining sharply at higher carbon. The figure supports the conclusion that carbon exceeding 1 wt.% in the aged state causes grain-boundary carbide precipitation, severely degrading toughness, whereas carbon below ~0.7 wt.% promotes δ-ferrite formation.

This figure presents a three-panel mechanical-property chart showing the effect of manganese content (24–34 wt%) on an Fe-Mn-Al-C austenitic steel containing fixed 9% Al and 0.9% C, water quenched from 1050°C and aged at 550°C for 16 h. The top panel plots ultimate tensile strength (UTS, solid triangles) and 0.2% yield strength (YS, open triangles) versus Mn content: UTS peaks near 1200 MPa at 26 wt% Mn and remains above 1100 MPa up to approximately 32 wt% Mn before falling to approximately 950 MPa at 34 wt% Mn, while 0.2% YS stays near 1000 MPa between 26 and 28 wt% Mn. The middle panel shows ductility indicators reduction of area (ψ, open circles) and elongation (δ, solid circles); ψ reaches approximately 60% at 30 wt% Mn and δ peaks near 45% at 30–31 wt% Mn, both declining sharply beyond 32 wt% Mn. The bottom panel gives U-notch impact toughness K_CU (open diamonds), which remains relatively stable at 100–115 J/cm² from 24 to 32 wt% Mn but collapses to approximately 35 J/cm² at 34 wt% Mn. The caption states that the degradation at high Mn is attributed to beta-Mn precipitation during aging, and the accompanying text reports that increasing Mn raises the solubility of Al and C in austenite, thereby reducing the amount of strengthening kappa-phase that precipitates during aging.
